title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: Identify potential external features on a fully autonomous vehicle (FAV) that help pedestrians understand the vehicle's intended behavior at a crosswalk, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior. The study addresses the communication gap between pedestrians and FAVs, as interpersonal communication is not possible with fully autonomous vehicles.
method_summary: Virtual reality experiment using HTC Vive headset with 30 participants walking across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Tested 4 visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' in text, flashing smile, control) and 4 audible features (horn, music, verbal message, control) in a 4x4 randomized complete block design. Collected objective measures (crossing time, waiting time from headset tracking) and subjective measures (feature ratings, receptivity questionnaires, pedestrian behavior questionnaire).
method_family: Virtual Reality Experiment, Randomized Complete Block Design
tasks: Pedestrian crossing behavior at virtual crosswalk; Feature preference assessment; Receptivity evaluation
metrics: Feature ratings (7-point safety scale); Crossing time; Waiting time; Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF); Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ); Personal Innovativeness Scale; Simulation Sickness Questionnaire
claim_text: Pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features (t=2.21, p<0.0349 for comfort; t=4.27, p<0.0002 for acceptance)
limitations: Study used a non-signalized intersection with a single controlled scenario where the FAV came from one side and stopped at the intersection | No other traffic was included in the virtual environment to eliminate distractions, limiting ecological validity | Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only; vehicle stopped in each trial - did not consider feature termination or vehicle restart signals | First exposure to FAV with features only - participants may behave differently with repeated exposure | Sample size of 30 participants limits generalizability; only tested specific feature combinations rather than all possible interactions
notes: VR validation study confirmed participants respond similarly in VR traffic environment as in real traffic. The walking silhouette is a familiar feature at pedestrian crossings, making it intuitive. Verbal message rated highest for audible features as it can break through distractions and help visually impaired pedestrians. Study used Unity 5 3D software with HTC Vive headset. Participants were US citizens, fluent English speakers with normal or corrected full-color vision.

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Pedestrians' red-light running behavior is one of the most critical factors for pedestrian-involved traffic crashes at intersections in China. The primary objective is to explore how various factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersection areas using data collected from Hefei, China.
method_summary: A questionnaire survey (SP survey) with 15 items was designed to collect pedestrians' socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attribute variables in different crossing facilities. Based on 631 valid samples collected from Hefei, China in May-June 2015, a binomial logistic model was established to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior. The Score test was used to validate significant relationships, and variables were included/excluded at the 95% confidence level.
tasks: identify factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior; predict probability of pedestrians' red-light running behavior at intersections
datasets: 631 valid pedestrian survey samples from Hefei, China (May-June 2015); Six typical signalized intersections in Hefei
metrics: Score test p-values; odds ratios; confidence intervals
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect the probability of pedestrians' red-light running behavior: trip purpose, time period in a day, pedestrian's attitude towards running a red light when in hurry, and pedestrian's attitude towards quality of road facilities
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei) | Sample size of 631 may not be sufficient for full generalization | Study only considered pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersection areas, not other crossing facilities | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons | Results may differ in other cities/countries due to differences in culture and driving behaviors
notes: The study addresses a significant traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The binomial logistic model provides a probability prediction function for red-light running behavior based on four significant variables. The research fills a gap by focusing specifically on Chinese pedestrian behavior and including attitude-related factors which were seldom considered in previous studies.

research_problem: Pedestrians' red-light running behavior is one of the most critical factors for pedestrian-involved traffic crashes at intersections in China. The research fills a gap by focusing specifically on Chinese pedestrian behavior and including attitude-related factors which were seldom considered in previous studies.

authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Pedestrians' red-light running behavior is one of the most critical factors for pedestrian-involved traffic crashes at intersections in China. claim_text: Pedestrians who indicate they will run a red light when in hurry are more likely to actually do so (odds ratio = 0.299 for non-hurry group)

notes: The study addresses a significant traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The binomial logistic model provides a probability prediction function for red-light running behavior based on four significant variables. The research fills a gap by focusing specifically on Chinese pedestrian behavior and including attitude-related factors which were seldom considered in previous studies.

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Pedestrians' red-light running behavior is one of the most critical factors for pedestrian-involved traffic crashes at intersections in China. The primary objective is to explore how various factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersection areas using data collected from Hefei, China.

The Score test was used to validate significant relationships, and variables were included/excluded at the 95% confidence level.
tasks: identify factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior; predict probability of pedestrians' red-light running behavior at intersections
datasets: 631 valid pedestrian survey samples from Hefei, China (May-June 2015); Six typical signalized intersections in Hefei
metrics: Score test p-values; odds ratios; confidence intervals
claim_text: Pedestrians whose crossing behavior is affected by road facility quality are more likely to run red lights at intersections (odds ratio = 0.483 for unaffected group)
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei) | Sample size of 631 may not be sufficient for full generalization | Study only considered pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersection areas, not other crossing facilities | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons | Results may differ in other cities/countries due to differences in culture and driving behaviors
notes: The study addresses a significant traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identifying personal and environmental factors affecting pedestrian red light running violations at signalized urban intersections to develop effective engineering, enforcement and educational initiatives
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal captured across 652 cycles. Random parameter logit model developed to measure association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors including pedestrian demographics, behaviors of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition.
tasks: Pedestrian red light running violation prediction and factor analysis
datasets: Video observation data from 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 6320 pedestrians observed during red time across 652 cycles
metrics: Akaike Information Criterion (AIC), restricted loglikelihood, unrestricted loglikelihood, chi-square statistics
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (coefficient = -0.433, odds ratio = 0.649)
limitations: Study focuses on red light running propensity without examining actual crash risk or safety outcomes | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior not assessed | Video observation cannot capture pedestrian safety perception, attitude, or anticipated crossing behavior - only behavioral outcomes observed | No pedestrian signal countdown device present at study sites - findings may not generalize to locations with countdown signals
notes: Study addresses gap in literature by simultaneously examining individual-level (pedestrian demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors. Novel contribution includes analysis of social norm effects (presence of companion, other pedestrians, other violators) on individual decision. Random parameter approach used to account for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian risk-taking attitudes.

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks

claim_text: Pedestrian age significantly affects red light running propensity: adolescents have lower propensity (coefficient = -0.228) while elderly have higher propensity (coefficient = 0.174) compared to adults
limitations: Study focuses on red light running propensity without examining actual crash risk or safety outcomes | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior not assessed | Video observation cannot capture pedestrian safety perception, attitude, or anticipated crossing behavior - only behavioral outcomes observed | No pedestrian signal countdown device present at study sites - findings may not generalize to locations with countdown signals

Random parameter approach used to account for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian risk-taking attitudes.

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identifying personal and environmental factors affecting pedestrian red light running violations at signalized urban intersections to develop effective engineering, enforcement and educational initiatives

metrics: Akaike Information Criterion (AIC), restricted loglikelihood, unrestricted loglikelihood, chi-square statistics
claim_text: Social influences significantly affect red light running: presence of a companion reduces propensity (coefficient = -0.984), increased number of pedestrians around reduces propensity (coefficient = -0.394), but presence of other violators increases propensity (coefficient = 1.029)
limitations: Study focuses on red light running propensity without examining actual crash risk or safety outcomes | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior not assessed | Video observation cannot capture pedestrian safety perception, attitude, or anticipated crossing behavior - only behavioral outcomes observed | No pedestrian signal countdown device present at study sites - findings may not generalize to locations with countdown signals

Novel contribution includes analysis of social norm effects (presence of companion, other pedestrians, other violators) on individual decision. Random parameter approach used to account for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian risk-taking attitudes.

claim_text: Signal time and traffic condition significantly affect red light running: longer time to green (coefficient = 0.090) and red time (coefficient = 0.060) increase propensity, while higher traffic volume (coefficient = -0.021) and more heavy vehicles (coefficient = -0.018) decrease propensity
limitations: Study focuses on red light running propensity without examining actual crash risk or safety outcomes | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior not assessed | Video observation cannot capture pedestrian safety perception, attitude, or anticipated crossing behavior - only behavioral outcomes observed | No pedestrian signal countdown device present at study sites - findings may not generalize to locations with countdown signals

Novel contribution includes analysis of social norm effects (presence of companion, other pedestrians, other violators) on individual decision. Random parameter approach used to account for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian risk-taking attitudes.

limitations: Study focuses on red light running propensity without examining actual crash risk or safety outcomes | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior not assessed | Video observation cannot capture pedestrian safety perception, attitude, or anticipated crossing behavior - only behavioral outcomes observed | No pedestrian signal countdown device present at study sites - findings may not generalize to locations with countdown signals

Novel contribution includes analysis of social norm effects (presence of companion, other pedestrians, other violators) on individual decision. Random parameter approach used to account for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian risk-taking attitudes.
